1: 'Elihu continued and said' — Elihu's fourth speech begins with the same wayyōsep̄ resumptive marker as Job 36:1 MT. This is his final extended argument, concluding with the meteorological hymn that will transition into YHWH's own whirlwind speech. Structurally, Elihu functions as both foil to the three friends (he arrives fresh after their failure) and as theological preparation for the divine answer. His speeches (Job 32-37) are widely regarded as a later addition to the core dialogue, but their literary placement is deliberate: they intensify the theophanic tension.
2: 'Bear with me a little longer (ṣĕʿar)' — the patience-request echoes the wisdom teacher's address to pupils who must wait for understanding to be complete. Elihu presents himself as God's advocate (v3: 'I will ascribe righteousness to my Maker'), a role that distinguishes him from the three friends who attempted to defend God by condemning Job. His claim that he 'still has something to say in God's defense' frames his speech as amicus curiae testimony in the divine court — the book's legal metaphor extends through his peroration.
3: 'I will draw my knowledge from a wide range (lĕmērāḥôq)' — from far away/afar; the image is of gathering knowledge from distant sources, perhaps implying cosmological or universal scope in contrast to the friends' limited retributive framework. 'Ascribe righteousness to my Maker' — this phrase anticipates Job's own eventual response in 40:8 where God challenges Job: 'Will you condemn me that you may be righteous?' Elihu already proposes what the divine speech will vindicate: that God's governance is righteous and can be seen as such from a sufficiently wide perspective.
4: 'For truly my words are not false; one complete in knowledge (tĕmîm dēʿôṯ) stands before you' — tāmîm (complete/blameless) applied to knowledge is unique here; elsewhere tāmîm describes moral integrity (Genesis 6:9 Noah; 17:1 Abraham; Psalms 15:2). Elihu's claim to be 'complete in knowledge' is a bold claim that many readers find arrogant — yet the divine speech partially vindicates Elihu in that it does not rebuke him (38:1 addresses Job directly; Job 42:7-9 rebukes only the three friends, not Elihu). Whether this constitutes validation of Elihu's claim is debated.
5: 'Behold, God is mighty (ḵabbîr) and despises no one' — ḵabbîr (mighty/great; from kābar, to be great/heavy) appears here and in v26 ('God is great, beyond knowing') framing the Elihu speech with divine greatness. The claim that God 'despises no one' (wĕlōʾ yimʾas) directly contradicts what Job has perceived: that God has rejected/despised him (Job 9:21; Job 30:22). Elihu is arguing that God's might is never arbitrary contempt but always purposeful governance. The Psalter uses the same term for the God who does not despise the afflicted (Psalms 22:24).
6: 'He does not keep the wicked alive, but gives justice (mišpāṭ) to the poor (ʿăniyyîm)' — the distributive justice schema: the wicked die; the poor receive legal protection. This is the classic Deuteronomic framework (Deuteronomy 10:18 'he executes justice for the fatherless and the widow') and the Psalter's repeated claim (Psalms 10:14-18; Job 146:7-9). Elihu is asserting that God's pattern, rightly understood, favors the vulnerable — which is meant to comfort Job but inadvertently classifies him as either wicked (dying) or not-yet-vindicated poor.
7: 'He does not take his eyes off the righteous (ṣaddîqîm); he seats them on the throne with kings and exalts them forever' — the double honor: divine surveillance (protective watching, not punitive scrutiny) and social elevation to royal dignity. The irony is that Job began in exactly this position (the divine attention of Job 1:8 'Have you considered my servant Job?') and now experiences it as catastrophic. Elihu is citing the theological principle; Job is living the theological exception that the principle cannot account for. The 'forever' (lāneṣaḥ) of their exaltation anticipates the restoration of Job 42.
8: 'And if they are bound in chains (bakkabbullîm), caught in ropes of affliction (ḥablê ʿōnî)' — the transition from honor (v7) to affliction signals Elihu's pastoral intent: even the righteous may be temporarily bound. The binding imagery appears throughout the Psalms of individual lament (Psalms 116:3 'cords of death entangled me'; Job 118:27 'bind the festival sacrifice with cords'). The chains (kabbul/kabbal) are literally 'fetters' — the metaphor is of divine discipline that temporarily restrains movement, not permanent condemnation.
9: 'Then he shows them what they have done and their transgressions — that they have acted arrogantly' — the purpose of the binding is pedagogical revelation: God exposes to the sufferer what motivated divine action. The word for 'acted arrogantly' (yitgabbārû, hitpa'el of gābar) is the same root used for a warrior's strength; here it means self-willed excess. Elihu's framework: suffering is God's tutorial that identifies specific behavioral patterns in the sufferer that need correction. This is Hebrews 12:5-11's 'discipline' (paideia) theology, which cites Proverbs 3:11-12 (itself drawing on this tradition).
10: 'He opens their ears (gilāh ʾāzĕnām) to correction' — gilāh ʾōzen (uncover/open the ear) is a prophetic revelation formula: 1 Samuel 9:15 (YHWH reveals to Samuel); 2 Samuel 7:27 (YHWH uncovers David's ear about the temple promise); Isaiah 50:4-5 (the Servant's ear opened by God each morning for instruction). Its use here by Elihu is significant: the instrument of divine communication is the ear-opening, not the thunderbolt or the voice in the whirlwind — though Job 38 will deploy that form too. The pedagogical opening precedes the storm disclosure.
11: 'If they listen and serve him, they will complete their days in prosperity (bĕṭôḇ) and their years in comfort (binnĕʿîmîm)' — the condition-consequence structure of the Wisdom tradition's two-ways framework: Proverbs 3:1-2 ('do not forget my law... so your days will be many and prosperous'); Deuteronomy 28:1-14 (the blessings for obedience). The temporal completeness ('complete their days') echoes the life-completion formula for the patriarchs (Genesis 25:8; Job 35:29 'full of years'). Elihu is not wrong about the general pattern — he is wrong to apply it as a simple diagnostic to Job's specific case.
12: 'But if they do not listen, they will perish by the sword (šelaḥ, = javelin/spear) and die without ever understanding (lĕḇaltî-ḏāʿaṯ)' — the two-ways formula completes: the path of refusal ends in violent death and intellectual darkness. lĕḇaltî-ḏāʿaṯ (without knowledge/understanding) is the opposite of the wisdom goal; Proverbs 5:23 'he dies for lack of discipline and is lost through his great folly.' The specific judgment instrument (sword/weapon) connects to the covenant curse vocabulary of Leviticus 26:25 and Deuteronomy 28:22.
13: 'The godless in heart (ḥanĕpê-lēḇ) store up resentment (śāmû ʾāp̄)' — ḥānēp̄ (godless/profane, one who is impious without necessarily being an external apostate) stores anger rather than submitting in affliction. The phrase śāmû ʾāp̄ (they place/store anger) is unique — the wicked nurture their resentment against God's correction instead of repenting. 'They do not cry to God when he binds them' — the failure of prayer as the marker of godlessness: the righteous cry to God even in extremity (Psalms 22:24; Job 34:15-18) while the godless maintain their self-sufficiency to the end.
14: 'They die young (bĕnaʿar), and their life ends among the cult prostitutes (qĕḏēšîm)' — the qĕḏēšîm (cult prostitutes, literally 'consecrated ones'; masculine form) appear in Deuteronomy 23:17-18 ('no Israelite man shall be a qāḏēš'), 1 Kings 14:24, and Job 15:12 (Josiah purging them from the land). The association of premature death with this category represents moral and cultic pollution — the ultimate social disgrace in the Deuteronomic framework. The verse is stark: the godless die young and die in shame.
15: 'But he rescues (ḥālaq, or better yĕḥallēṣ = delivers) the afflicted through their suffering (ʿōnî) and opens their ears through oppression (lĕlaḥaṣ)' — the counter-trajectory: for the humble, the very suffering that would destroy the godless becomes the instrument of salvation. The verb ḥālaṣ (to draw out/deliver/pull free) appears in the Exodus vocabulary (Psalms 81:6) and in the individual rescue psalms (Psalms 34:7 'angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear him and delivers them'). Elihu's theological point here is close to Isaiah 53:4-5 (through his suffering he bore their afflictions).
16: 'Indeed, he was drawing you from the jaws of distress (ḥāṣar ṣār) into a broad place (rĕḥāḇāh)' — the 'broad place' (rĕḥāḇāh, spaciousness/freedom from constraint) is the standard Psalms-vocabulary for divine rescue from distress: Psalms 18:19 'he brought me out into a broad place'; Psalms 31:8; Job 118:5 'out of my distress (min-hammēṣar) I called on the LORD; he answered and set me in a broad place (bammerḥāḇ).' Elihu is applying to Job the Psalms of rescue in the present tense — insisting that the rescue process is underway, not that Job has been abandoned.
17: 'But now you are full of the judgment due the wicked; judgment and justice have seized you' — Elihu's sharp pivot: Job's current situation appears to fulfill the formula for wickedness. The observation is empirically accurate (Job suffers) but theologically misapplied (Job is not wicked). The verse exposes the structural problem of retributive theology: it reads providential patterns backwards from outcomes — seeing suffering and concluding guilt. The book's resolution in Job 42 will reverse this reading entirely, vindicating Job precisely as a righteous sufferer.
18: 'Beware that anger (śĕḥōq, = mockery/laughing) does not lead you into scoffing' — the warning against turning from patient endurance to bitter mockery of God. The fear that suffering, when it does not resolve into the expected pattern, will produce theological cynicism — the 'scoffers' (lēṣîm) of Proverbs who mock the wisdom framework itself (Proverbs 1:22; Job 3:34; Job 19:29). 'Do not let the greatness of the required payment (ḵōp̄er, ransom) turn you away' — ḵōp̄er (ransom/redemption price) here refers to the scale of what Job perceives is owed; the ransom metaphor connects to the redemption vocabulary of Exodus.
19: 'Will your wealth (šûʿăṯĕḵā, = outcry/riches) hold you in distress?' — the ironic question: all that Job once possessed (the great wealth of Job 1) cannot maintain him now, cannot purchase the outcome he desires. The wisdom tradition consistently warns against trusting in wealth as a buffer against divine judgment: Proverbs 11:4 'wealth does not profit in the day of wrath'; Job 11:28 'whoever trusts in riches will fall.' Job has already made this point himself — his wealth is gone and proved unable to save him.
20: 'Do not long for the night (lĕlayyĕlāh) when people vanish from where they stand' — the 'night' here may refer to death (a common metaphor: Psalms 30:5 'weeping stays for the night'; John 9:4 'night comes when no one can work') or to the chaotic darkness before divine intervention. The warning against wishing for death is a pastoral note: Job has explicitly desired death throughout (Job 3:3-13; Job 6:8-9; Job 14:13). Elihu warns that this longing itself is spiritually dangerous — a turning from God rather than toward him.
21: 'Be careful not to turn toward evil, for this is what you have been choosing over enduring affliction' — the accusation that Job's theological challenges to God are themselves a choice of evil over patient suffering. This is the most pointed of Elihu's charges and the most debatable: the book's prologue has already established Job's integrity, and God's eventual speech does not vindicate Elihu's specific accusation against Job. However, the general principle — that impatient protest can shade into genuine rebellion — is a real spiritual danger the text takes seriously.
22: 'Behold, God is exalted in his power; who is a teacher (môreh) like him?' — môreh (teacher) is a rare divine epithet; it appears as a proper title only here and in Isaiah 30:20 ('your Teacher will no longer hide himself; your eyes will see your Teacher'). Isaiah 30:20's placement in a restoration oracle suggests that YHWH's role as direct Teacher of his people is an eschatological expectation — the age when the prophetic mediator is no longer needed because God himself teaches. Jesus's reception as teacher (rabbi) and the Spirit's role as the teaching Paraclete (John 14:26) fill this office in the NT.
23: 'Who has prescribed (p̄āqad) his path for him? Who can say, You have done wrong?' — p̄āqad (to assign/commission/appoint) used here as prescribing God's route: no one can direct the divine governance. The question recurs in Isaiah 40:13 ('Who has directed the Spirit of the LORD or instructed him as his counselor?') — Paul quotes Isaiah 40:13 in Romans 11:34 and 1 Corinthians 2:16. The logic of both Isaiah and Elihu is the same: divine incomprehensibility implies that God cannot be called to account by the creature. Paul draws the further conclusion: that incomprehensibility is itself gracious news (Romans 11:33-36).
24: 'Remember to extol his work, which people have sung about through the ages' — the command to praise as the appropriate human response to divine incomprehensibility. The hymnic tradition (mĕšōrĕrîm = those who sing/hymn) represents the accumulated human witness to God's works across generations: the Psalter itself is this tradition. The call to 'remember' (zĕḵar) and 'extol' (śĕgāʾ) connects to the Psalms of orientation that celebrate divine works: Psalms 77:11 'I will remember the deeds of the LORD'; Psalms 111:2 'great are the works of the LORD; they are pondered by all who delight in them.'
25: 'All people have seen it (ḥāzāh); everyone looks at it (yāḇîṭ) from a distance' — the universal accessibility of the divine work in creation as a theological premise: every human being has some capacity to perceive what God does in the world. This is the premise of natural theology (Romans 1:19-20 'since the creation of the world, God's invisible qualities... have been clearly seen'). The 'from a distance' (mērāḥôq) may acknowledge the epistemic limit: created beings can see the works but not comprehend the Worker — the same limit that Job's cosmological questions have approached.
26: 'Behold, God is great, beyond our knowing (lōʾ-nēḏāʿ); the number of his years cannot be counted' — lōʾ-nēḏāʿ is a remarkable predicate: God is incomprehensible. The claim that divine years 'cannot be counted' (wĕlōʾ yēḥqer) uses the same root (ḥāqar) as Job's accusation in Job 5:27 ('we have searched this out; it is true'); now applied to divine eternity itself as beyond searching. The year-metaphor connects to Psalms 90:2 ('from everlasting to everlasting, you are God') and Revelation 1:8 ('the Alpha and Omega... who is and was and is to come'). Paul's doxology in 1 Timothy 6:16 ('unapproachable light... no one has seen or can see') draws on this tradition.
27: 'For he draws up the drops of water (yigra ʿnṭpê-māyim); they distill as rain from the mist' — the meteorological cycle described: YHWH draws up moisture from the surface waters (evaporation), which condenses and falls as rain. The physical accuracy of the description is notable: the Hebrew verb gāraʿ (to diminish/draw off) captures the drawing-away of moisture from surfaces. This is an early articulation of the hydrological cycle — not as scientific observation but as a theological claim: even the invisible mechanics of weather are divine governance.
28: 'Which the clouds pour down (yizzĕlû) and drop on people in abundance' — the cascade from vapor to cloud to rain as a series of divine actions, each step a new distributive act. The theological point is that rain is not a natural process operating independently but a continuous divine provision: Deuteronomy 11:14 ('I will send rain on your land in its season'); Matthew 5:45 ('he sends rain on the righteous and unrighteous'). The word nāzal (to flow/trickle down) is the same used in Isaiah 45:8 for righteousness 'showering down from the clouds' — meteorological and ethical provisions in one image.
29: 'Can anyone understand the spreading of the clouds (mip̄rĕśê ʿāḇ) or the thunder from his canopy (śûkkāh)?' — the rhetorical question form that will dominate Job 38 appears here in Elihu's speech as a preview. śukkāh (canopy/booth/tent) is the sacred tent vocabulary: the divine 'booth' as the protective overhead structure from which thunder comes. The same root covers the Sukkot booths of Leviticus 23:42 and the 'shelter' of Psalms 27:5 ('he will hide me in his sukkah'). God's meteorological dwelling is structurally analogous to his covenantal dwelling-place.
30: 'See, he scatters his lightning (bĕrāqāyw) around him and covers the depths of the sea' — the global scope of lightning as divine communication: it is not random but 'scattered' (p̄ārāś) from the divine center outward in every direction. The 'covering the depths of the sea' clause (wĕšārĕšê hayyām ḵissāh) is difficult; it may describe how lightning illuminates to the ocean's roots, revealing what is normally hidden. This cosmic lightning imagery appears in Psalms 77:18 (theophanic psalm: 'your lightnings lit up the world; the earth trembled and quaked') and Psalms 97:4 ('his lightning lit up the world').
31: 'For by these (bām = by/with them) he governs peoples and provides food in abundance' — the political and agricultural dual function of storms: the same meteorological power that destroys (judgment on nations) provides sustenance (rain for crops). The connection between rain and governance appears throughout the OT: Deuteronomy 28:23-24 (no rain as covenant curse); 1 Kings 17-18 (Elijah's drought as prophetic sign against Ahab); Jeremiah 5:24 ('they do not say in their hearts, Let us fear the LORD our God, who gives the autumn and spring rains'). The nations are governed by the same mechanism that feeds them.
32: 'He covers his hands with lightning (ʿal-kappayim kissāh ʾôr) and commands it to strike its mark (wayĕṣawwehû ʿal-maprîaʿ)' — the image of God wielding lightning as a weapon held in covered hands that are revealed at the moment of striking is a remarkable anthropomorphism. The divine hands (kappayim) appear throughout the Psalter as agents of salvation (Psalms 16:11; Job 31:5 'into your hand I commit my spirit') and judgment (Psalms 76:5). Lightning as a divine arrow is explicit in Psalms 18:14 ('he shot his arrows and scattered them; great bolts of lightning and routed them').
33: 'Its thunder (raʿm) announces his presence (miqneh = regarding what is coming); the cattle also sense the coming storm' — the verse functions as the final note of Job 36's meteorological hymn: even the cattle register the approach of the divine storm, their instinctual response being a form of creaturely acknowledgment. The juxtaposition of human argument (vv2-21) and animal instinct (v33) sets up the contrast that YHWH will exploit in Job 38-39: the animals 'know' God's ordering by instinct while Job's sophisticated theological arguments miss what the creatures grasp.
